executive_summary.description: A high-level overview of the procurement technology landscape in the nine specified mid-sized US cities. It summarizes the prevalence of e-procurement portals and the general lack of cross-jurisdictional search tools and advanced AI features.

key_findings.description: A bulleted list of the most important takeaways from the research, including the dominant platforms, the use of separate compliance systems, and the gap between user needs and current system capabilities.

city_name.description: Name of the city (e.g., Richmond, Baltimore).

primary_procurement_platform.description: The main e-procurement platform or portal used by the city for solicitations.

platform_provider.description: The company that provides the procurement platform (e.g., OpenGov, Bonfire, Workday).

vendor_portal_url.description: The direct URL for the city's vendor or supplier portal.

compliance_system.description: Any separate system used for vendor compliance or supplier diversity tracking (e.g., B2Gnow).

operational_status.description: The current status of the system (e.g., Live, Sunset).

notes_on_functionality.description: Observations on the system's features, particularly regarding contract search capabilities and limitations.

city_procurement_technology_profiles.description: Detailed profiles for each specified city (Richmond, Baltimore, Pittsburgh, Louisville, Norfolk, Raleigh, Durham, Chattanooga, New Orleans). Each profile will detail the primary procurement platform(s) used (e.g., OpenGov, Bonfire, Workday, BRASS), vendor portals, compliance systems, operational status (live/sunset), key features, and an assessment of its ability to reduce manual contract searching.

platform_name.description: The name of the commercial e-procurement platform (e.g., OpenGov Procurement, Bonfire).

provider_company.description: The company that develops and sells the platform.

core_functionalities.description: A description of the platform's main features, such as bid posting, vendor management, and electronic submissions.

cities_using_platform.description: A list of the surveyed cities that utilize this platform.

common_eprocurement_platform_analysis.description: An analysis of the major commercial e-procurement platforms identified across the cities, such as OpenGov, Bonfire, and Workday. For each platform, this section will detail its core functionalities and which of the surveyed cities utilize it.

system_name.description: The name of the compliance or diversity management system.

provider_company.description: The company that provides the system (e.g., B2Gnow).

purpose.description: The primary function of the system, such as tracking MBE/WBE participation or local hiring compliance.

cities_using_system.description: A list of the surveyed cities that have implemented this system (e.g., Baltimore, Richmond).

supplier_diversity_and_compliance_system.description: Information on specialized systems used for tracking vendor compliance and supplier diversity (e.g., MBE/WBE programs). This will detail the purpose of systems like B2Gnow and identify the cities (e.g., Baltimore, Richmond) that have implemented them.

cross_jurisdictional_contract_search_ana.description: An analysis of whether the identified city-level systems support searching for valid contracts across different governmental levels (city, state, federal). This section directly addresses the core user problem, highlighting current fragmentation and limitations.

ai_ocr_and_advanced_analytics_assessment.description: An assessment of the adoption of advanced technologies like AI-powered contract analysis, OCR for data extraction, and procurement intelligence dashboards. This will note the presence or general absence of these specific tools in the surveyed cities.

platform_name.description: The name of the Contract Lifecycle Management (CLM) platform (e.g., CobbleStone Contract Insight).

provider_company.description: The company that provides the CLM software (e.g., CobbleStone Systems Corp.).

implementing_agency.description: The specific government agency or department using the platform (e.g., Virginia Information Technologies Agency - VITA).

role_in_procurement.description: Description of how the platform is used to manage contracts post-award.

portal_name.description: The official name of the state or regional procurement portal (e.g., eVA, NC eProcurement).

jurisdiction.description: The state or region the portal serves (e.g., Virginia, North Carolina).

portal_url.description: The URL for the procurement portal.

relevance_to_cities.description: How the portal is used by or impacts the procurement processes of the surveyed cities.

state_and_regional_procurement_portals.description: Details on relevant state-level or regional e-procurement marketplaces, such as Virginia's eVA and NC eProcurement, and their relevance to procurement staff in the target cities.

system_name.description: The name of the custom or less common procurement system (e.g., BRASS, BidNet).

city.description: The city that uses this system.

system_type.description: The type of system (e.g., Custom-built, Niche COTS).

description.description: A description of the system's unique features and functionalities.

system_implementation_status_overview.description: A summary of the operational status (e.g., Live, Sunset, In Development) of the various procurement systems across all nine cities, directly addressing a key part of the user's request.

primary_platform.description: The main e-procurement platform used by the city.

vendor_compliance_system_identified.description: The name of the vendor compliance system, if one was identified (e.g., B2Gnow, N/A).

challenges_in_procurement_modernization.description: A discussion of the common challenges and limitations observed in municipal procurement technology, such as data fragmentation, lack of interoperability between systems, and budget constraints.

vendor_registration_and_portals_overview.description: An overview of how vendors typically register and interact with the cities through various supplier portals, including iSupplier, Workday portals, and platform-specific registration pages.

future_outlook.description: A brief outlook on potential future trends in civic procurement technology for mid-sized cities, such as the potential for increased platform consolidation, integration with financial systems, and the slow adoption of advanced analytics.
